title: Lagging the hot water cylinder and supply pipes
subtitle: Cylinder jacket, pipe insulation, and the loft run
excerpt: An unlagged hot water cylinder loses around 1 kWh of heat per day to the surrounding air. A fitted jacket cuts that to under 200 Wh. Pipe lagging the first 1.5 m of hot supply adds further. Total materials cost around ; payback is inside two heating seasons.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust cylinder insulation guidance. EST quoted saving: cylinder jacket saves approximately 90 kWh/year at 2025/26 cap rates (gas 6p/kWh), around .50/year directly, but the bigger saving is faster hot water recovery on an immersion. Pipe lagging guidance from BSI PD 7190 simplified heat loss tables.

content.text: A hot water cylinder in an unheated airing cupboard or loft can lose 1-2 kWh per day, running continuously around the clock whether you are drawing hot water or not. A British Standard 80 mm thick cylinder jacket cuts that standby loss by about 75%. Combined with lagging the first 1.5 m of each hot supply pipe, the total heat saved is modest in absolute terms but costs almost nothing to achieve.
What you need
Cylinder jacket, BS 1566 standard, 80 mm thick glass fibre. Sold in small, medium, and large; measure the cylinder height and diameter before buying.

Pipe lagging foam tubes, sized to the pipe outside diameter (usually 15 mm or 22 mm for domestic supply pipes). Buy 2 m lengths; a slot runs along the tube for fitting over pipes without disconnecting them.
Tape, adhesive tape or cable ties to hold tube joints and prevent the lagging from sliding.
Sharp utility knife or scissors for cutting foam tubes to length.
Cylinder jacket
Step 1, clear the airing cupboard.
Remove anything stored against the cylinder. Check for any existing jacket, a thin pre-fitted jacket (often 25 mm) can be left in place and a new 80 mm jacket fitted on top.
Step 2, fit the jacket sections.

Most cylinder jackets come in two or four segments. Wrap the segments around the cylinder, aligning the zip or tie points. Ensure the jacket fits flush at the top and bottom and is not bridging any immersion heater boss or thermostat pocket, those need access.
Step 3, tie the segments.
Fasten the ties or straps so the segments are snug against the cylinder. There should be no gaps at the seams; air pockets at the seam allow convective heat loss.
Pipe lagging
Step 4, lag the hot supply pipes.

Cut foam tube sections to length with a utility knife. Open each section along its slit and press it over the pipe. Lag the first 1.5 m of each hot pipe run from the cylinder top, this is where the heat loss per metre is highest. Also lag the cold feed pipe from the cylinder base down for 300 mm to prevent the heat rising back down by convection.
Step 5, seal the joins.
At tees, bends, and valves, cut V-mitres in the foam tube so sections meet without gaps. Tape the joints or use cable ties every 300 mm on long runs.
Loft pipes

Any pipe that runs through an unheated loft, cold-water feed, overflow, expansion, needs lagging to prevent freezing as much as to conserve heat. Lag the full length of any loft run, with particular care at the eaves where the insulation level drops and wind chill is highest.

items.fix: Check jacket thickness is 80 mm; fit an additional 25 mm layer over any visible gaps at seams.
items.cause: Jacket too thin (25 mm) or seams not sealed
items.symptom: Cylinder still warm to the touch through the jacket

items.fix: Cut the tube at the bend, fit two straight sections meeting at a mitre, tape the join.
items.cause: Bend radius too sharp for the foam thickness
items.symptom: Foam tube splits at a tight bend
